question: Describe the key differences between a stack and a queue. Provide a real-world example of an application where each would be appropriately used.
expectedPoints: A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) structure, while a queue is a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) structure.
Example for a stack: The undo/redo functionality in a text editor, where the last action is the first to be undone.
Example for a queue: A print job spooler, where documents are printed in the order they were sent to the printer.

question: Explain the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). Why is maintaining the BST property crucial, and what is the time complexity of this operation in a balanced vs. an unbalanced tree?
expectedPoints: The new node is inserted as a leaf by comparing its key with the root and recursively traversing left (if smaller) or right (if larger) until a null child is found.
Maintaining the BST property (left subtree < root < right subtree) is crucial for efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations.
Time complexity is O(log n) on average for a balanced tree but degrades to O(n) in the worst-case scenario of an unbalanced, linear tree.

question: Compare and contrast the adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations for graphs. Analyze the trade-offs in terms of time complexity for common operations (checking for an edge, finding all neighbors) and space complexity, providing specific scenarios where one representation would be preferred over the other.
expectedPoints: An adjacency matrix uses a V x V array, allowing O(1) edge lookup but consuming O(V²) space. An adjacency list uses an array of lists, consuming O(V + E) space but requiring O(degree(V)) to find all neighbors of a vertex.
Checking for an edge (u, v) is O(1) in a matrix but O(degree(u)) or O(log(degree(u))) in a list (if sorted). Finding all neighbors of v is O(V) in a matrix but O(degree(v)) in a list.
A matrix is preferred for dense graphs with many edges, where frequent edge lookups are needed. A list is preferred for sparse graphs and when memory is a constraint, as it is more space-efficient.
